headline: US blockade of Iranian ports enters second day as Trump claims Tehran 'wants a deal' despite failed peace talks.

sections.label: Situation Overview
sections.title: US Blockade Takes Effect as Diplomatic Efforts Stall
paragraphs: The United States military blockade of Iranian ports entered its second full day today, with little traffic entering or leaving Iranian ports as the measure takes hold. President Donald Trump announced the blockade began at 10am ET on Monday after weekend peace talks in Pakistan failed to reach agreement.

Trump claimed today that Iranian officials had contacted the US and 'want to work a deal', though he did not specify who made contact. Iran accused the US of committing 'piracy' as thousands rallied in Tehran against the blockade, which targets shipping through the strategic Strait of Hormuz.
key_facts: US blockade of Iranian ports began Monday at 10am ET
Weekend peace talks in Pakistan ended without agreement after 21 hours
Trump threatens to destroy Iranian 'fast attack ships' approaching blockade
Iran's IRGC warns of retaliation with 'new forms of warfare'
sections.australia_relevance: Australia imports 90% of its liquid fuel and is highly vulnerable to oil price spikes from Middle East disruptions.

sections.label: Background
sections.title: Six-Week War Escalates Despite Ceasefire
paragraphs: The conflict began on February 28 when the US and Israel launched air strikes against Iran, killing its supreme leader and other officials while destroying military and government targets. Iran retaliated with missile and drone strikes against Israel, US bases, and US-allied countries, while effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz.

A two-week ceasefire announced April 8 remains technically in effect until April 22, but has been violated by both sides. The conflict has expanded to include a separate war between Israel and Hezbollah in southern Lebanon, complicating peace efforts.
key_facts: War began February 28 with US-Israeli strikes on Iran
Iran's supreme leader and other officials killed in initial attacks
Strait of Hormuz effectively closed, disrupting 20% of global oil transit
Over 2,000 killed in parallel Israel-Hezbollah conflict in Lebanon
sections.australia_relevance: Australia has joined international calls for restraint while working with allies to secure alternative fuel supplies.

sections.label: Diplomacy
sections.title: International Mediation Efforts Continue
paragraphs: Pakistan has offered to host a second round of US-Iran negotiations this week, despite the failure of weekend talks in Islamabad. UN Secretary-General António Guterres called for talks to continue, emphasising there is no military solution to the conflict.

Russia has repeated its offer to accept Iran's enriched uranium as part of a potential agreement, while Qatar's Foreign Minister urged both sides to engage constructively in mediation efforts. China's Xi Jinping called for the world not to 'revert to the law of the jungle' during talks with Abu Dhabi's crown prince.
key_facts: Pakistan offers to host second round of talks this week
Russia offers to accept Iran's enriched uranium stockpiles
UN calls for preservation of two-week ceasefire
Israel-Lebanon talks ongoing in Washington despite Hezbollah rejection
sections.australia_relevance: Australia supports multilateral diplomatic efforts and has endorsed UN calls for immediate ceasefire.

sections.label: Economy & Energy
sections.title: Oil Prices Surge as Australian Energy Costs Spike
paragraphs: Oil prices jumped 4.3% today with Brent crude reaching $99.36 per barrel, up over 31% since the war began. Australian petrol prices have hit record highs of $2.38 per litre, with fuel supplies potentially running low by month's end as Asian refineries exhaust inventories.

Qantas warned its jet fuel bill could rise 32% or $800 million, while Westpac increased credit provisions anticipating customer difficulties from higher energy costs. Reserve Bank Deputy Governor Andrew Hauser warned of 'stagflationary shock' - rising inflation with slowing growth.
key_facts: Brent crude oil up 31% since war began, reaching $99.36 per barrel
Australian petrol prices hit record $2.38 per litre
Qantas faces potential $800 million increase in fuel costs
Westpac increases bad debt provisions due to energy price impact
sections.australia_relevance: Energy price shocks threaten Australian inflation targets and economic growth, with RBA considering policy responses.

sections.label: What to Watch
sections.title: Critical Week Ahead for Conflict Resolution
paragraphs: The effectiveness of the US blockade will become clearer as only 24 ships passed through the Strait of Hormuz last week, compared to 135 daily before the war. Iran estimates war damages at $270 billion and is demanding reparations as part of any peace deal.

The April 22 ceasefire deadline approaches with Israel-Lebanon talks continuing in Washington despite Hezbollah's rejection. Markets are closely watching whether promised second-round US-Iran talks materialise, with Deutsche Bank analysts citing 'growing hopes' for a deal.
key_facts: April 22 ceasefire deadline approaching rapidly
Iran seeks $270 billion in war reparations from US and Israel
Potential second round of Pakistan-mediated talks this week
20,000 seafarers remain stranded on ships in Persian Gulf
sections.australia_relevance: Australian fuel security depends on Strait of Hormuz reopening, with current reserves providing only 36 days of petrol supply.
